title: Launching many containers in parallel produces: Error: saving container $containerid state: beginning container $containerid save transaction: database is locked
body:
### Issue Description

In Red Hat Ansible Controller (downstream of https://github.com/ansible/awx ), Controller launches jobs via receptor (https://github.com/ansible/receptor ) which really ends up launching podman containers. When many jobs are launched concurrently, we see errors like:

```

Error: beginning container create transaction: database is locked
```
or 
```
Error: preparing container 8871268197e3d8325752aae4ae11c5578d46e32319fc0ba6bef6e3a47c0c6268 for attach: saving container 8871268197e3d8325752aae4ae11c5578d46e32319fc0ba6bef6e3a47c0c6268 state: beginning container 8871268197e3d8325752aae4ae11c5578d46e32319fc0ba6bef6e3a47c0c6268 save transaction: database is locked
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue


This is reproducible without awx/receptor etc. All you need to do is start many containers concurrently that will run a command for some duration of time (60 seconds in this case).

A minimal reproducer script is:

```
#!/bin/bash

# Number of containers
TOTAL=800

# Base image
IMAGE=alpine

# Pull image first to avoid delays
podman pull $IMAGE

# Create containers in background
for i in $(seq 1 $TOTAL); do
  podman run -d --name test_ctr_$i --replace $IMAGE sleep 60 &
done

# Wait for all podman processes to finish launching
wait

echo "Launched $TOTAL containers."
```


### Describe the results you received

In the output, you will see that some containers fail like this:
```
Error: cleaning up container 517fb3ce94c60e2b9773e9f4457ff199a5526b99e7249210b1efe771be207133: beginning transaction to remove old timestamps: database is locked
Error: beginning container create transaction: database is locked
cd09c871bda3c946a54e297590758e722584233a4968699edccd4bb69783ac89
9953c99cb7d651c15296c850ebe3b3e3b594f8d96346e8c753256bd89320df1e
23f79fda80f30ce63b7b2fdef3c7067e4f8c35661b5f0ae32b729ae012ee7955
Error: saving container 8e5bfc7f57a63ff3898ff8ff5f74f5fd0e2efe00bb328f480dfe4f0df4dc9347 state: beginning container 8e5bfc7f57a63ff3898ff8ff5f74f5fd0e2efe00bb328f480dfe4f0df4dc9347 save transaction: database is locked
5ac38389187233e4fd119e659bcd9ed03dd124724f688e726b75ddef184c7558
Error: beginning container create transaction: database is locked
9c0e88e9972bb4e2ab951e6395a56ab6260e3730de4c960b310aefcc3232526b
0c6ede1f5b76cca00e5613c7536dac9e3ea154fefe4cf740c4a147331c69ab67
Error: saving container 259899752a2c089500bb5fd7162bde1e5535db33923367f66bb9edf4e04ec61e state: beginning container 259899752a2c089500bb5fd7162bde1e5535db33923367f66bb9edf4e04ec61e save transaction: database is locked
```

### Describe the results you expected

Podman will throttle itself if needbe in starting the containers if access to its database is limited

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless (?)

### Upstream Latest Release
```
$ podman --version
podman version 4.9.4-dev
```

### Additional environment details

This is on a larger machine
```
$ lscpu
Architecture:            x86_64
  CPU op-mode(s):        32-bit, 64-bit
```

### Additional information

comments.body: We only support the most recent version upstream. Please try with Podman 5.4.1 and see if you can reproduce. I know we've had several SQLite bugfixes since the 4.9 release series.

It is how sqlite works in our design, I don't think anything would have changed since https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20838.  Fundamentally I don't think starting 800 containers in parallel is exactly a use case we can (or even should) handle with the current podman design. Sure we can bump the busy timeout even further but if the process is waiting over 100s for the lock I don't think podman will be usable very much anyway. 


(But yes we only support the latest version, if you have the problems in the Red Hat versions you must reported them through the Red Hat channels instead)

@Luap99 We did have some initialization hickups initializing the database that I know caused very similar errors, so I do think there's a potential this was fixed (or at least partially alleviated).

Newer Podman does expose an environment variable to increase database lock timeouts (`PODMAN_SQLITE_BUSY_TIMEOUT`) that might help here as well? But I wouldn't lean on that too heavily, and as @Luap99 said, you could end up waiting a very, very long time for the lock.

FWIW I was not able to reproduce on my local machine w/ 5.4.1, but I think part of the issue is running this on much larger machine that is just able to go faster. I think:

> Fundamentally I don't think starting 800 containers in parallel is exactly a use case we can (or even should) handle with the current podman design

Is the important point to bring back to my team to make them think about constraining the concurrency at which they start containers.

Thank you for the comments!
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I'm going to also chime in and say I consistently see issues with containers being started concurrently at much lower levels than @kdelee (think 3-4). and I dont agree that 'its not a use case podman could handle' since most of the issues I'm seeing look like race conditions, not just timeouts from a db query.

@james-lawrence 3-4 is quite difference from 800. All I said that podman is not designed to handle several of hundreds   containers in parallel.

If you see that on 3-4 containers please provider a reproducer and how long do the commands take? It is certainly possible that there are ways in sqlite that trigger these errors by just using it in parallel somehow.

I dont see the precise same issue unfortunately. mine seems to be around what looks like container corruption where containers individual images become completely unable to start. think i filed an issue regarding it, been swamped I'll eventually roll back around and double check.

for the timeout issue that looks like podman is relying solely on sqlite timeouts to handle the concurrency and there should probably be a semophore limitation on the number of concurrent startups which would resolve the original issue in this thread. while still allowing 800 containers to spin up ;) *eventually*
